XSLT

XSLT

The Extensible Stylesheet Language for Transformations (XSLT) is an XML-based language for expressing transformations to the structure of XML streams. Given a well-formed XML stream as input, XSLT can be used to construct an output in XML or other character-based output formats.

You can use XSLT to transform XML into HTML for display on the Web or text formats, such as comma-separated lists. It is also commonly used to transform XML into a new XML structure. This includes:

  • Altering nodes in the XML stream. For example, converting elements to attributes, or adding or removing a node.
  • Changing the precedence of items in the XML stream. For example, sorting the content of an XML stream alphabetically.

Uniface provides a component API (USYSXSLT) to transform XML streams at run-time. You can edit and test XSLT files using the XSLT Workbench.

How XSLT Works

XSLT is a rule-based language. An XSLT processor matches patterns in the input XML stream with rules in the XSLT stylesheet, and applies an output template specified by the rule. For example, to find an attribute named myAttribute and output the attribute’s value in an element named <myElement>, you could use the following template:

<xsl:template match="@myAttribute"> <!--Apply this template to myAttribute. -->
 <xsl:element name="myElement"> <!--Create element myElement. -->
 <xsl:value-of select="."/> <!--Insert
the value of myAttribute.-->
 </xsl:element>
</xsl:template> <!--End
of template.-->

This XSLT template creates the following fragment of XML:

...<myElement>AttributeValue</myElement>...

Learning More About XSLT

XSLT is a rich language with many statements and functions. The language is unique in several respects, such as it’s treatment of variables, the data types allowed, and the handling of processing flow. As such it is recommended to consult specialist sources devoted to XSLT.

The specification for XSLT is issued by the World Wide Web Consortium.

For examples of XSLT stylesheets, see XMLTRANSFORM.

Related Links
  • XSLT

    The Extensible Stylesheet Language for Transformations. A W3C recommendation that defines a declarative syntax for expressing the relationship between an input XML structure and an output structure. An XSLT processor can then transform an input XML stream into the output structure. The output structure can be XML, HTML, or text. XSLT is an XML grammar, and includes the XPath W3C recommendation.
